story:
I’m holed up in my ramshackle farmhouse by the lake, down where the White Mountains start to rise, drinking cheap beer before the lunch bell like that’s a plan. Out here among the pines, the alien invaders—folks call them ABs, short for something not polite—haven’t bothered with me yet. But it’s coming. Any day now I can picture the door splitting, the clatter of tiny feet up the stairs, big bug eyes in my bedroom, me too scared to move while they drag me out. So I type to pass the time and hold onto that stubborn little thing we keep calling hope. Remember when we thought E.T. was cute?

Turn on a screen, twist a dial, scroll a feed—every channel still limping along runs AB news all day and night. We talk, rage, plan, riot, deploy troops, drop bombs, argue and post and scream ourselves hoarse, and the ABs don’t flinch. Nothing touches them. Isn’t it strange, how together we are in hating them, and how useless that togetherness feels? Remember when enemies bled and retreated? When force meant something? Those were the “good old days,” we say, like that helps.

Meanwhile, the local folks are gathering in my barn. Rumor says if you sneak behind an AB, go low, and drive a pitchfork up fast, you can drop one. Sounds like a drunk fisherman’s tale. Still, the rumor spreads, because what else do we have? “There’s always hope,” we say, the way people say “bless you” after a sneeze—automatic, well-meant, maybe magical if you squint.

Hope’s a stubborn habit. We keep telling our old stories: Gideon’s tiny band against a sea of enemies; half-shod patriots outlasting the British Empire; Marines raising the flag on Iwo Jima when all seemed lost. The history’s fuzzy in the retelling, but the point lands the same—underdogs sometimes win. If once, maybe again. If maybe, then maybe tonight.

Some of the locals swear they’ve seen bright lights dancing above the ridge. “Has to be an AB base,” they say, and by midnight they’ll storm it—with pitchforks, mind you, the kind that were made for hay, not war. I laugh because it’s ridiculous, and I don’t laugh because maybe ridiculous is all we’ve got left. Out here, thinking too long just circles back to the front door breaking.

So what do I do? Finish another six-pack and pass out? Sit and wait to be plucked like a chicken? Or pick up the old pitchfork and step into the dark with the rest of them, not because it’s smart, but because you can only hide so long before you have to choose what kind of fool you’ll be—the fool who believes in nothing, or the fool who shows up. I tell myself I’ll go—go out in a blaze of something, if not glory, then at least company. Stranger things have happened, right? And if hope is a habit, tonight’s as good a night as any to practice it.
